narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd like—specifically, a 65% chance of not passing. Picture it like a story of your academic journey so far: your Year 3 grades landed you in the 7th decile at school, which stood out as the biggest red flag, signaling to the model that your performance has hovered in the middle of the pack when it counts most toward the end. That concern echoed back to your Year 1 decile of 5th, reinforcing a pattern of steady but not standout results that the model linked strongly to bar struggles. Your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, surprisingly tipped the scales toward caution in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting patterns where similar backgrounds don't always translate to the grit needed for bar success. Your LSAT score of 37 added to that narrative, painting you as capable but not quite at the elite threshold that often predicts passage. Even your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.1 and white racial background pulled in the same direction, suggesting the model sees these as part of a profile that's historically faced hurdles. Your choice to go full-time nudged things further toward risk, while being male was the one bright spot, gently pulling toward success. It's this combination—like chapters in a book that don't quite build to a triumphant close—that led the model to predict failure more likely than not, prompting us to protect our program's bar passage rates. But here's the empowering part: this isn't your final chapter; boosting those deciles through targeted study or retakes could rewrite the story entirely. You've got the full-time commitment—lean into it, and let's see you shine next time. We're rooting for you.
